question: A 45-year-old female patient with triple-negative breast cancer (cT2 cN0 cM0) is receiving guideline-directed neoadjuvant chemo-immunotherapy according to the KEYNOTE-522 regimen (4 cycles of dose-dense EC every 2 weeks, followed by weekly Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab every 3 weeks). Following the completion of the EC phase and two weeks into the Carboplatin/Paclitaxel phase, a sonographic follow-up shows no change in tumor size (Stable Disease). The patient is clinically stable and asymptomatic. Which therapeutic recommendation is most appropriate in this situation?

options.A: Immediate discontinuation of neoadjuvant therapy and performance of primary surgery (mastectomy or breast-conserving therapy), as this represents primary treatment failure.
options.B: Continuation of the planned chemo-immunotherapy (Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab) until the completion of the scheduled 24 weeks, followed by surgical intervention.
options.C: Switching the regimen to a "salvage" therapy with Capecitabine or Eribulin, given the insufficient response during the anthracycline-based phase.
options.D: Addition of radiation therapy during the ongoing chemotherapy to improve local control prior to surgery.
options.E: Multidisciplinary re-evaluation with repeat imaging and biopsy only if true progression is suspected; otherwise continuation of the planned neoadjuvant regimen.

question: TA 45-year-old woman with triple-negative breast cancer (cT2 cN0 cM0) completes the full KEYNOTE-522 neoadjuvant regimen (dose-dense EC followed by carboplatin/paclitaxel plus pembrolizumab). She undergoes breast-conserving surgery and sentinel lymph node biopsy. The pathology report shows residual invasive tumor of 8 mm (ypT1b ypN0), classified as RCB-II. What is the most appropriate evidence-based adjuvant systemic treatment?

options.A: Continuation of Pembrolizumab alone for 9 additional cycles to complete the one-year course.
options.B: Discontinuation of Pembrolizumab and initiation of 6–8 cycles of adjuvant Capecitabine (CREATE-X protocol).
options.C: Continuation of Pembrolizumab for 9 additional cycles in combination with 6–8 cycles of adjuvant Capecitabine.
options.D: Switch to Olaparib for one year, regardless of the patient's BRCA mutation status.
options.E: No further systemic therapy is indicated because the residual tumor is small (8 mm, ypT1bN0); proceed with radiotherapy and routine follow-up only.

question: In the Phase III DESTINY-Breast11 trial evaluating neoadjuvant strategies for patients with high-risk, HER2-positive early breast cancer (eBC), which of the following statements best describes the primary efficacy results and the clinical implications of the T-DXd-containing experimental arm?

options.A: T-DXd monotherapy (8 cycles) demonstrated a statistically superior pCR rate compared to ddAC-THP, establishing it as a new anthracycline-free standard of care for neoadjuvant HER2+ breast cancer.
options.B: Sequential T-DXd followed by THP (4+4 cycles) achieved a pCR rate of 67.3%, a statistically significant improvement of 11.2% over standard ddAC-THP in the DESTINY-Breast11 neoadjuvant trial.
options.C: While T-DXd-THP improved pCR rates, the benefit was restricted solely to the hormone receptor-negative subgroup, with no significant difference observed in hormone receptor-positive patients.
options.D: The T-DXd-THP regimen was associated with a higher incidence of Grade ≥3 adverse events and left ventricular dysfunction compared to the anthracycline-based control arm in this trial.
options.E: Early event-free survival data showed a hazard ratio of 0.85, indicating the pCR benefit has not yet translated into a trend toward improved long-term clinical outcomes in this trial.

question: What was the primary reason for the early closure of T-DXd monotherapy arm in the DESTINY-Breast11 trial?

options.A: A change in the standard of care to T-DM1 during the trial
options.B: high rates of Grade 3 febrile neutropenia
options.C: excessive pulmonary toxicity (ILD/pneumonitis)
options.D: futility in surpassing the pCR rate of the standard ddAC-THP arm
options.E: poor patient recruitment for the monotherapy cohort

question: Regarding the safety profile in DESTINY-Breast11, how did the T-DXd followed by THP arm compared to the standard ddAC-THP arm in terms of toxicity?

options.A: there was no significant difference in cardiotoxicity between arms
options.B: ddAC-THP showed a better overall safety profile compared to T-Dxd, with fewer serious adverse events (10.2% vs 20.2%)
options.C: Left ventricular dysfunction was significantly less frequent in the T-DXd-THP arm
options.D: Cardiotoxicity was only observed in patients receiving radiotherapy
options.E: T-DXd-THP had a higher rate of congestive heart failure

question: A 62-year-old postmenopausal patient with de novo metastatic HR+/HER2- breast cancer (bone and lung metastases) is starting first-line therapy. She is otherwise healthy but is concerned about long-term survival and potential gastrointestinal side effects, as she has a history of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). Which of the following statements best reflects the current evidence-based consensus regarding the choice of a CDK4/6 inhibitor for this patient?

options.A: Palbociclib should be the preferred choice because the PALOMA-2 trial showed the highest statistically significant overall survival (OS) benefit in postmenopausal women when combined with Letrozole.
options.B: Ribociclib is currently the only CDK4/6 inhibitor with statistically significant OS benefit demonstrated across three separate Phase III trials (MONALEESA-2, -3, and -7), including first-line, second-line, and premenopausal settings.
options.C: Abemaciclib should be avoided solely due to her lung metastases, as the MONARCH-3 trial showed no benefit in patients with visceral involvement.
options.D: All three CDK4/6 inhibitors are considered interchangeable in terms of Overall Survival, and the choice should be made based only on the patient's preference for the dosing schedule.
options.E: Abemaciclib should be the preferred agent because its continuous dosing schedule produces more sustained CDK4/6 pathway suppression; diarrhea is dose-dependent and manageable with loperamide, so a history of IBS is not a clinically relevant contraindication to its use.

question: A 63-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer (bone-only disease) received letrozole plus palbociclib as first-line therapy and achieved stable disease for 26 months.She now has asymptomatic radiologic progression confined to bone.Repeat biopsy confirms ER+/HER2− disease. ctDNA analysis shows: newly acquired ESR1 Y537S mutation, no RB1 alteration, no CCNE1 amplification, no PIK3CA mutation, no AKT1 mutation, intact PTEN, low Ki-67 (15%). Which of the following is the most appropriate next treatment?

options.A: Fulvestrant monotherapy
options.B: Elacestrant monotherapy
options.C: Fulvestrant plus ribociclib
options.D: Fulvestrant plus abemaciclib
options.E: Capecitabine

question: A 61-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer (bone and small-volume nodal disease) has been receiving letrozole plus palbociclib as first-line therapy for 11 months. She achieved a partial response and remains asymptomatic.Serial ctDNA monitoring (every 2 months) shows: - Month 0: No ESR1 mutation detected - Month 6: ESR1 Y537S detected at 0.2% VAF - Month 8: 0.8% VAF - Month 10: 3.4% VAF Imaging at month 10 shows continued partial response by RECIST. Tumor markers have risen slightly but remain within normal range. No RB1 mutation. No PIK3CA mutation. No CCNE1 amplification. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Continue letrozole plus palbociclib until radiologic progression
options.B: Switch to fulvestrant plus palbociclib
options.C: Switch to elacestrant monotherapy
options.D: Switch to fulvestrant plus abemaciclib
options.E: Discontinue CDK4/6 inhibition and start capecitabine

question: A 64-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer (bone-only disease) has been receiving letrozole plus ribociclib for 16 months as first-line therapy. She achieved a partial response and remains asymptomatic. Recent evaluation: - CT scan: Stable disease by RECIST (minor increase in two bone lesions, not meeting progression criteria) in CT scan. tumor markers gradually increase over 4 months but still <1.5× ULN. ECOG 0 Serial ctDNA (every 3 months): Month 9: ESR1 Y537S detected at 0.3% VA, month 12: 0.6%, month 15: 1.1%. no PIK3CA mutation. Which is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Continue letrozole plus ribociclib and repeat imaging in 8–10 weeks to assess trajectory before considering any therapy change.
options.B: Switch to fulvestrant plus ribociclib now, given prolonged stable disease without evidence of further deepening of response on current therapy.
options.C: Switch to elacestrant monotherapy to proactively address possible emerging ESR1-mediated endocrine resistance before overt clinical progression.
options.D: Switch to fulvestrant plus abemaciclib to achieve improved progression-free survival outcomes based on available cross-trial comparisons.
options.E: Start capecitabine as single-agent chemotherapy given the clearly insufficient response observed with the current endocrine-based combination regimen.

question: A 49-year-old premenopausal woman with HR+/HER2− invasive ductal carcinoma initially presents with: Clinical tumor size: 4.5 cm, clinically positive axillary lymph nodes, grade 3, Ki-67: 35%. At surgery, pathology reveals: non pCR with a residual invasive tumor 2.1 cm , 2 of 14 lymph nodes positive, ER 90%, PR 30%, HER2 negative. She begins ovarian suppression plus an aromatase inhibitor. Which of the following statements regarding adjuvant CDK4/6 inhibitor therapy is most accurate?

options.A: CDK4/6 inhibitors are not indicated because only 2 nodes are positive post-treatment
options.B: Abemaciclib is indicated based on high-risk clinicopathologic features
options.C: Ribociclib is not appropriate because NATALEE excluded patients receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy
options.D: Palbociclib is appropriate because residual disease predicts CDK4/6 sensitivity
options.E: Both abemaciclib and ribociclib are evidence-based options in this scenario

question: A 66-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with: cT2N1 HR+/HER2− invasive ductal carcinoma, tumor size: 3.4 cm, clinically positive axillary node (biopsy-proven), grade 2, ER 95%, PR 70%, HER2 negative, Ki-67: 29%. Because of strong ER expression, she is started on a 3-week neoadjuvant letrozole window before surgery. At 3 weeks, repeat core biopsy shows: Ki-67: 26% (no meaningful suppression). She remains clinically stable. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Continue neoadjuvant endocrine therapy for 1-2 weeks and repeat Ki-67 biopsy in 2 weeks before making a decision
options.B: Switch to neoadjuvant anthracycline/taxane-based chemotherapy
options.C: Proceed directly to surgery and determine adjuvant therapy based on final pathology
options.D: Add a CDK4/6 inhibitor to endocrine therapy in the neoadjuvant setting (off label use)
options.E: Perform genomic testing (Oncotype DX) before deciding on systemic escalation

question: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman has cT2N1 HR+/HER2− invasive ductal carcinoma (ER 95%, PR 50%, HER2 negative, grade 2, Ki-67 22%). After neoadjuvant anthracycline/taxane chemotherapy, surgery reveals residual invasive tumor 2.8 cm with 3 of 16 positive lymph nodes. She will start an aromatase inhibitor. Past medical history: well-controlled atrial fibrillation on apixaban, baseline QTc 470 ms, chronic IBS with intermittent diarrhea, mild CKD (eGFR 55), ECOG 0. She meets monarchE criteria for adjuvant CDK4/6 therapy. Which CDK4/6 inhibitor is most appropriate?

options.A: Abemaciclib for 2 years
options.B: Ribociclib for 3 years
options.C: Palbociclib for 2 years
options.D: No CDK4/6 inhibitor due to comorbidities
options.E: Capecitabine instead of CDK4/6

question: A 65-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2− breast cancer completed 2 years of adjuvant abemaciclib and continues on letrozole. Eighteen months after finishing abemaciclib she develops multiple bone metastases and two small liver lesions. ECOG 0. Liver biopsy confirms HR+/HER2-low disease. ctDNA shows ESR1 Y537S mutation, no RB1 mutation, no CCNE1 amplification, no PIK3CA mutation. What is the most appropriate first-line metastatic treatment?

options.A: Elacestrant monotherapy
options.B: Letrozole + ribociclib
options.C: Fulvestrant + ribociclib
options.D: Trastuzumab deruxtecan
options.E: capecitabine

question: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2-low metastatic breast cancer and ESR1 Y537S mutation received fulvestrant and ribociclib as first-line metastatic therapy. After 9 months, imaging shows enlargement of two liver lesions (largest 2.8 cm) with stable bone disease, no hyperbilirubinemia, mildly rising AST/ALT (1.5× ULN), no visceral crisis, ECOG 0. Repeated ctDNA shows persistent ESR1 mutation, no RB1 mutation, no CCNE1 amplification. What is the most appropriate next treatment?

options.A: Continue fulvestrant plus ribociclib, as CDK4/6 inhibitor rechallenge with the same regimen is supported by emerging data after first-line progression.
options.B: Initiate trastuzumab deruxtecan based on HER2-low status, as DESTINY-Breast04 showed OS benefit over standard chemotherapy after endocrine therapy failure.
options.C: Switch to everolimus plus exemestane, as mTOR inhibition can restore endocrine sensitivity after CDK4/6 inhibitor progression in ESR1-mutant HR+/HER2− disease.
options.D: Initiate elacestrant monotherapy, as EMERALD trial data support its use in ESR1-mutant HR+/HER2− disease progressing after CDK4/6 inhibitor-based endocrine therapy.
options.E: Start capecitabine chemotherapy, as progression on fulvestrant plus ribociclib confirms endocrine resistance and cytotoxic chemotherapy is the most appropriate next step.

question: A 59-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer presents with progression after first-line letrozole + ribociclib (PFS 24 months) bone and limited liver metastases. ECOG 0\. Repeat ctDNA reveals: PIK3CA H1047R mutation, no ESR1 mutation, no RB1 mutation, no CCNE1 amplification. She has well-controlled type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.4%). What is the most appropriate next-line therapy?

options.A: Elacestrant monotherapy
options.B: Fulvestrant + alpelisib
options.C: Fulvestrant + abemaciclib
options.D: Exemestane + everolimus
options.E: Fulvestrant + Inavolisib + Palbociclib

question: A 61-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer develops progression after first-line Letrozole + Ribociclib (18 months benefit). She has bone and liver metastases and also a HbA1c: 6.8% (prediabetes). NGS (ctDNA) shows: PIK3CA E545K mutation, PTEN truncating mutation, no ESR1 mutation. She is asymptomatic and endocrine-eligible. Which of the following is the most appropriate next-line targeted therapy strategy?

options.A: Alpelisib + Fulvestrant, because PIK3CA mutation is present
options.B: Capivasertib + Fulvestrant, because PTEN alteration predicts benefit from AKT inhibition
options.C: Everolimus + Exemestane, because dual pathway mutations predict resistance to PI3K inhibition
options.D: Switch to chemotherapy because dual pathway activation predicts endocrine resistance
options.E: Switch CDK4/6 inhibitor and endocrine partner, e.g. Abemaciclib + Fulvestrant

question: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer progressed after first-line letrozole plus ribociclib. ctDNA reveals an ESR1 Y537S mutation and no PIK3CA mutation. She has bone and lymph node metastases without visceral crisis. HbA1c is 5.8%. ECOG 0.Which of the following statements is the most accurate regarding optimal next-line therapy?

options.A: Fulvestrant monotherapy is preferred after CDK4/6 inhibitor progression in ESR1-mutant disease, as ESR1 mutations paradoxically increase sensitivity to parenteral selective estrogen receptor degraders
options.B: Elacestrant demonstrated superior progression-free survival over standard endocrine therapy specifically in ESR1-mutant HR+/HER2− metastatic breast cancer after CDK4/6 inhibitor-based treatment in the EMERALD trial
options.C: Exemestane plus everolimus is the preferred regimen after CDK4/6 inhibitor progression in ESR1-mutant disease, as mTOR inhibition specifically restores endocrine sensitivity in tumors with ESR1 ligand-binding domain mutations
options.D: ESR1 mutations confer resistance to all ER-targeting agents including oral SERDs, making endocrine therapy futile and chemotherapy the only appropriate next-line option
options.E: Alpelisib plus fulvestrant is appropriate regardless of PIK3CA status, as CDK4/6 inhibitor resistance universally activates PI3K signaling making PIK3CA testing unnecessary

question: Which of the following most accurately defines HER2-low in metastatic breast cancer?

options.A: Tumors demonstrating HER2 immunohistochemistry (IHC) 1+ or 2+ with negative in situ hybridization (ISH), in the absence of HER2 gene amplification
options.B: Tumors with HER2 IHC 2+ regardless of ISH status, including cases with low-level HER2 gene copy number gain without meeting amplification criteria
options.C: Tumors with HER2 IHC 0 or 1+ and equivocal ISH results that do not fulfill ASCO/CAP criteria for HER2 positivity
options.D: Tumors with HER2 IHC 2+ and HER2/CEP17 ratio <2.0 but average HER2 copy number ≥ 6.0 signals per cell
options.E: Tumors with heterogeneous HER2 expression showing focal IHC 3+ staining in <10% of tumor cells and negative ISH

question: Which statement most accurately reflects the current biological understanding of HER2-low breast cancer?

options.A: HER2-low tumors have a distinct ERBB2 somatic mutational profile that differentiates them biologically from true HER2-0 tumors on genomic sequencing.
options.B: HER2-low tumors represent a stable intrinsic molecular subtype that is biologically distinct from HER2-negative disease on gene expression profiling.
options.C: HER2-low status is primarily a predictive biomarker for antibody-drug conjugates rather than a biologically distinct entity with consistent molecular features.
options.D: HER2-low tumors demonstrate consistent HER2 mRNA overexpression relative to HER2-0 tumors that is reproducibly detectable across multiple gene expression platforms.
options.E: HER2-low classification is driven by HER2 gene amplification below the ISH positivity threshold rather than by protein expression on immunohistochemistry.

question: A patient with metastatic HR-positive breast cancer was initially classified as HER2 IHC 0 on primary tumor biopsy. A metastatic biopsy 3 years later is IHC 1+, ISH-negative. Which statement best explains the clinical relevance?

options.A: HER2 status conversion from negative to 1+ confirms clonal evolution to HER2-driven disease requiring targeted therapy.
options.B: The patient becomes newly eligible for trastuzumab plus pertuzumab dual HER2 blockade combination.
options.C: The patient may now be eligible for trastuzumab deruxtecan based on newly confirmed HER2-low expression.
options.D: HER2 reclassification to IHC 1+ has no therapeutic implication without concurrent gene amplification on ISH.
options.E: IHC 1+ result requires mandatory reflex ISH testing to confirm eligibility for ADC-based therapy.

question: Why can trastuzumab deruxtecan demonstrate activity in HER2-low tumors despite minimal HER2 expression?

options.A: It induces antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity independent of HER2 receptor expression density.
options.B: It binds ERBB2-mutated receptors with significantly higher affinity than wild-type HER2 receptors.
options.C: It activates HER2 downstream signaling to trigger compensatory apoptotic feedback in tumor cells.
options.D: It converts HER2-low tumors into HER2-amplified clones via epigenetic receptor upregulation.
options.E: It exerts a bystander effect via membrane-permeable payload release into the surrounding tumor microenvironment.

question: A 58-year-old woman presents with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer. Pathology revealed: HER2 IHC 1+ (faint incomplete membranous staining in 40% of tumor cells), ISH negative (ratio 1.2; copy number 2.8). Prior therapy: neoadjuvant anthracycline/taxane. First-line metastatic therapy: taxane + pembrolizumab. Progression after 8 months.No prior antibody–drug conjugate exposure.The tumor board is discussing the role of trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd).Which of the following is the most accurate and guideline-concordant statement?

options.A: T-DXd is contraindicated in TNBC as HER2 amplification on ISH is required for efficacy; IHC 1+ with negative ISH does not constitute a valid treatment indication.
options.B: T-DXd is appropriate as randomized data demonstrated overall survival benefit in HER2-low metastatic disease including the HR-negative subgroup after at least one prior chemotherapy line.
options.C: T-DXd should only be used after progression on sacituzumab govitecan, as prospective sequencing data demonstrate superior outcomes when sacituzumab is administered before T-DXd in TNBC.
options.D: T-DXd requires confirmatory repeat HER2 testing using quantitative digital pathology before initiation, as standard IHC assessment is insufficient to establish HER2-low eligibility reliably.
options.E: T-DXd benefit in HER2-low disease appears restricted to HR-positive tumors, as the HR-negative subgroup showed no statistically significant benefit in the pivotal randomized trial data.

question: A 61-year-old woman has metastatic triple-negative breast cancer. Tumor characteristics: HER2 IHC 1+ (faint incomplete membranous staining in 30% of cells), ISH negative, PD-L1 CPS 15\. Treatment history: - Neoadjuvant anthracycline/taxane - First-line metastatic: pembrolizumab + taxane → progression after 9 months - No prior antibody–drug conjugate exposure - Mild pre-existing interstitial lung abnormalities on CT (asymptomatic) The tumor board is discussing second-line therapy and sequencing strategy between: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-Dxd) vs. Sacituzumab govitecan (SG). Which of the following is the most evidence-based and biologically sound statement regarding sequencing?

options.A: Sacituzumab govitecan should be given first because randomized data demonstrate superior overall survival compared with trastuzumab deruxtecan in the HER2-low TNBC population.
options.B: Trastuzumab deruxtecan should be preferred first because HER2-low status is a stronger and more validated predictive biomarker than Trop-2 expression for ADC selection.
options.C: There are no head-to-head sequencing data; choice should integrate biomarker expression, toxicity profiles including ILD risk, prior therapies, and patient-specific risk factors.
options.D: Sacituzumab govitecan must precede trastuzumab deruxtecan because both share a topoisomerase I inhibitor payload and cross-resistance between them is considered universal.
options.E: Trop-2 expression testing is mandatory before sacituzumab govitecan but not before trastuzumab deruxtecan, as HER2 IHC alone determines eligibility for the latter agent.

question: A 38-year-old patient with stage II TNBC has already started neoadjuvant anthracycline followed by taxane. PD-L1 testing was not performed. She has completed 2 cycles of anthracycline and is tolerating therapy well. Which of the following is the most evidence-based approach?

options.A: Pembrolizumab cannot be added until PD-L1 testing results are known.
options.B: PD-L1 testing is required first; pembrolizumab is only indicated if CPS is ≥10.
options.C: Add pembrolizumab as the validated immunotherapy backbone for all TNBC patients.
options.D: Add pembrolizumab regardless of PD-L1 status and incorporate carboplatin during the taxane phase.
options.E: Switch to platinum plus pembrolizumab backbone and omit the anthracycline phase entirely.

question: A 41-year-old woman is diagnosed with stage I TNBC (cT1cN0, tumor 1.6 cm). Germline testing performed upfront reveals a pathogenic BRCA1 mutation. No comorbidities. ECOG 0\. No nodal involvement on imaging Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment strategy, and should she receive adjuvant olaparib?

options.A: Neoadjuvant platinum plus pembrolizumab, followed by adjuvant pembrolizumab and olaparib maintenance.
options.B: Upfront surgery followed by adjuvant chemotherapy and olaparib meeting by OlympiA criteria.
options.C: Upfront surgery followed by adjuvant olaparib alone, given the substantially elevated recurrence risk conferred by BRCA1 mutation.
options.D: Pembrolizumab-based neoadjuvant therapy; TNBC benefits from immunotherapy regardless of pathologic stage.
options.E: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by surgery; adjuvant olaparib is not indicated in stage I.

question: A 39-year-old woman presents with cT2N0 TNBC (3.1 cm). Germline testing upfront: pathogenic BRCA1 mutation. She receives neoadjuvant anthracycline/platinum-taxane-based chemotherapy in combination with pembrolizumab. Undergoes surgery and achieves pathologic complete response (ypT0N0). The tumor board discusses adjuvant systemic therapy. Which of the following is the most evidence-based recommendation?

options.A: Continue adjuvant pembrolizumab to complete 1 year; adjuvant olaparib is not indicated after achieving pathologic complete response.
options.B: Discontinue pembrolizumab and start adjuvant olaparib because germline BRCA1 mutation confers high-risk biology regardless of pathologic response.
options.C: Continue pembrolizumab and add adjuvant olaparib because dual pathway suppression reduces recurrence risk even after achieving pathologic complete response.
options.D: Replace pembrolizumab with adjuvant capecitabine because achieving pathologic complete response negates the remaining clinical benefit of continued immunotherapy.
options.E: Stop all systemic therapy because pathologic complete response eliminates clinically meaningful recurrence risk in germline BRCA1-mutated TNBC.

question: A 63-year-old postmenopausal woman is diagnosed with ER-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer with bone-only disease. She started on first-line letrozole. Restaging at 4 months shows new liver metastases. Which of the following scenarios best fulfills the formal definition of primary endocrine resistance according to international consensus criteria?

options.A: Recurrence while on adjuvant tamoxifen after 3 years of therapy
options.B: Recurrence 18 months after completing 5 years of adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy
options.C: Disease progression within 6 months of starting first-line endocrine therapy for metastatic disease
options.D: Disease progression after 14 months of clinical benefit on first-line fulvestrant in the metastatic setting
options.E: Recurrence during adjuvant endocrine therapy after 18 months of treatment

question: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman with ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer received adjuvant anastrozole for 5 years following surgery and radiation for stage II disease. Twenty months after completing endocrine therapy, she presents with bone and lymph node metastases. Biopsy confirms ER-positive, HER2-negative disease. Which of the following scenarios best meets the formal definition of secondary endocrine resistance according to international consensus criteria?

options.A: Recurrence during the first 18 months of adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy
options.B: Recurrence after 30 months of adjuvant tamoxifen therapy while still receiving treatment
options.C: Recurrence 20 months after completing 5 years of adjuvant endocrine therapy
options.D: Progressive disease 4 months after starting first-line letrozole for metastatic disease
options.E: Progressive disease 5 months after starting first-line fulvestrant for metastatic disease

question: According to international consensus criteria (ABC/ESMO), which of the following scenarios meet the definition of secondary endocrine resistance?

options.A: Recurrence at 18 months while receiving adjuvant aromatase inhitior treatment
options.B: Recurrence at 28 months while receiving adjuvant aromatase inhibitor therapy
options.C: Recurrence 14 months after completing 5 years of adjuvant endocrine therapy
options.D: Progressive disease 4 months after initiating first-line fulvestrant for metastatic disease
options.E: Recurrence at 22 months while receiving adjuvant aromatase tamoxifen monotherapy

question: Which of the following scenarios best fulfills the definition of visceral crisis according to contemporary international oncology guidelines (e.g., ABC/ESMO)?

options.A: A patient with multiple asymptomatic liver metastases occupying >50% of hepatic volume and mildly elevated transaminases (1.8× ULN), ECOG 1
options.B: A patient with lymphangitic carcinomatosis causing rapidly worsening dyspnea over 10 days, resting oxygen saturation 88% on room air
options.C: A patient with three new brain metastases and mild headaches but no neurologic deficits
options.D: A patient with progressive bone metastases and severe pain requiring opioid escalation
options.E: A patient with rising CA 15-3 levels and radiologic progression in the liver but normal liver function tests

question: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic hormone receptor–positive, HER2-negative breast cancer was diagnosed 18 months ago with liver and bone metastases. She received first-line letrozole plus ribociclib with an initial partial response lasting 14 months. Over the past 3 weeks, she reports rapidly worsening fatigue, abdominal discomfort, and new jaundice. Dyspnea has mildly increased but oxygen saturation is 95% on room air. Examination reveals scleral icterus and tender hepatomegaly. Examination reveals scleral icterus and tender hepatomegaly. Laboratory results: total bilirubin: 6.2 mg/dL (previously 1.1 mg/dL 3 weeks ago), AST/ALT: 5× ULN, ALP: markedly elevated, INR: 1.8, albumin: 2.8 g/dL, hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dLplatelets: 210,000/µL. CT imaging shows diffuse progression of hepatic metastases without biliary obstruction. No CNS disease. ECOG 2\. She discontinued ribociclib 10 days ago due to progression. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate systemic treatment strategy?

options.A: Switch to fulvestrant plus a different CDK4/6 inhibitor
options.B: Initiate elacestrant (oral SERD) based on likely ESR1 mutation
options.C: Start capecitabine monotherapy
options.D: initiate combination cytotoxic chemotherapy (e.g., taxane-based regimen)
options.E: switch to exemestan and everolimus

question: A 58-year-old woman with HR-positive, HER2-low (IHC 1+), metastatic breast cancer progressed after first-line letrozole + ribociclib (14 months benefit) and second-line fulvestrant + alpelisib (6 months benefit). Now she presents with rapidly progressive jaundice and fatigue over 2 weeks. Laboratory results: bilirubin: 5.6 mg/dL (previously 1.3 mg/dL 3 weeks ago), AST/ALT: 4× ULN, INR: 1.6, albumin: 2.8 g/dL, platelets: 180,000/µL. CT: diffuse hepatic progression without obstruction. ECOG 2 Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate systemic therapy?

options.A: Trastuzumab deruxtecan based on HER2-low (IHC 1+) status
options.B: Sacituzumab govitecan targeting Trop-2 expression
options.C: Weekly paclitaxel as standard single-agent chemotherapy
options.D: Platinum-based combination chemotherapy for BRCA-mutated disease
options.E: Elacestrant monotherapy for endocrine-resistant disease

question: A 51-year-old woman with no prior cancer history presents with progressive fatigue, abdominal distension, and jaundice evolving over 2–3 weeks. Imaging reveals a 3.2 cm breast mass, diffuse bilateral liver metastases but no biliary obstruction. Biopsy confirms ER 90%, PR 60%, HER2-negative (0 by IHC) invasive ductal carcinoma. Laboratory results: total bilirubin: 4.6 mg/dL (normal 3 weeks earlier), AST/ALT: 4× ULN, INR: 1.5, albumin: 2.9 g/dL, platelets: 220,000/µL, creatinine: normal. ECOG performance status: 2\. She is diagnosed with de novo metastatic HR+/HER2− breast cancer with hepatic visceral crisis. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial systemic treatment?

options.A: Letrozole plus ribociclib
options.B: Fulvestrant plus ribociclib
options.C: Upfront Sacituzumab Govitecan
options.D: Combination cytotoxic chemotherapy
options.E: Single-agent weekly paclitaxel

question: The 51-year-old woman presents with de novo metastatic ER 90%, PR 60%, HER2 0 invasive ductal carcinoma with diffuse liver metastases and hepatic visceral crisis (bilirubin 4.6 mg/dL, INR 1.5). She receives dose-adjusted combination carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy with rapid clinical improvement. After 3 cycles: bilirubin normalizes, transaminases decrease to <1.5× ULN. ECOG improves to 1\. Imaging shows partial response She remains chemotherapy-naïve prior to this episode. What is the most appropriate next systemic treatment?

options.A: Continue combination chemotherapy until maximal response (3 cycles more)
options.B: Switch to maintenance capecitabine
options.C: Start aromatase inhibitor + CDK4/6 inhibitor
options.D: Start sacituzumab govitecan
options.E: Start trastuzumab deruxtecan

question: A 50-year-old woman presents with de novo metastatic ER 90%, PR 60%, HER2 0 (IHC 0\) invasive ductal carcinoma with diffuse liver metastases and hepatic visceral crisis.She receives dose-adjusted weekly paclitaxel with rapid biochemical and radiologic improvement. After 4 cycles Bilirubin normalized. ECOG 1, partial response on imaging. Chemotherapy is stopped and she is started on letrozole + ribociclib.After 14 months, she develops hepatic progression. Repeat liver biopsy shows: ER 80%, PR 20%, HER2 IHC 1+ (ISH negative). liquid biopsy: ESR1 mutation detected. What is the most appropriate next-line therapy?

options.A: Fulvestrant + alpelisib
options.B: Elacestrant
options.C: Capecitabine
options.D: Trastuzumab deruxtecan
options.E: Sacituzumab govitecan

question: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic ER-positive (70%), PR-negative, HER2 0 breast cancer initially presented with liver and bone metastases. Treatment history: first-line AI + CDK4/6 inhibitor progression at 11 months, second-line fulvestrant + alpelisib (PIK3CA-mutant), progression at 8 months, third-line Capecitabine, progression at 6 months, fourth-line Sacituzumab govitecan with progression after 5 months. She now presents with worsening liver metastases and rising bilirubin (2.2 mg/dL), ECOG 1\. Repeat biopsy confirms: ER 30%,PR 0%, HER2 0\. no actionable mutations detected in liquid biopsy. What is the most appropriate next systemic therapy?

options.A: Elacestrant
options.B: Gemcitabin + Carboplatin
options.C: Trastuzumab Deruxtecan
options.D: Datopomab Deruxtecan
options.E: Eribulin

question: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with metastatic recurrence 4 years after completing adjuvant trastuzumab-based chemotherapy for stage II HER2-positive, ER-positive breast cancer. Current findings:ER 95%, PR 10%, HER2 IHC 3+, Liver and multiple bone metastases, no CNS disease, asymptomatic, normal laboratory results. She strongly wishes to avoid chemotherapy due to prior toxicity and is concerned about alopecia and neuropathy. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line therapy?

options.A: Trastuzumab + pertuzumab + aromatase inhibitor
options.B: Tratuzumab emtansine monotherapy
options.C: Trastuzumab + aromatase inhibitor alone
options.D: Trastuzumab + pertuzumab + CDK4/6 inhibitor + aromatase inhibitor
options.E: Trastuzumab + pertuzumab + paclitaxel

question: A 58-year-old woman with HR+/HER2+ metastatic breast cancer received first-line trastuzumab + pertuzumab + docetaxel for liver and bone metastases. She achieved a partial response lasting 14 months and now has radiographic progression in the liver. Clinical details: ECOG 1, no CNA metastasis, LVEF 58%, mild residual grade 1 neuropathy. Pulmonary function: normal. What is the most appropriate second-line therapy?

options.A: Trastuzumab emtansin
options.B: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd)
options.C: Tucatinib + trastuzumab + capecitabine
options.D: Trastuzumab + lapatinib
options.E: Trastuzumab + endocrine therapy

question: A 49-year-old premenopausal woman was diagnosed with stage IIIB HR+/HER2+ breast cancer. She received neoadjuvant TCHP, residual disease at surgery, adjuvant T-DM1 for 14 cycles. 8 months after completing adjuvant T-DM1, she develops multiple liver metastases and bone lesions. Liver metastasis biopsy revealed: ER 80%, PR 10% HER2 IHC 3+ What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for metastatic disease?

options.A: Trastuzumab + pertuzumab + taxane
options.B: Trastuzumab + pertuzumab + endocrine therapy
options.C: Trastuzuman emtansine
options.D: Trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd)
options.E: Dapotomab deruxtecan

question: A 48-year-old premenopausal woman with HR+/HER2+ metastatic breast cancer is receiving trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) as second-line therapy after prior trastuzumab/pertuzumab + taxane. She achieved a partial response lasting 11 months. Current restaging shows two enlarging liver lesions (2.1 cm and 2.4 cm), all other prior liver lesions decreased or stable. Stable bone disease. No new lesions. No CNS metastases. No ILD. ECOG 0\. LVEF 60%. CA 15-3 stable. What is the most appropriate nexgt management step?

options.A: Continue T-DXd unchanged and repeat imaging in six weeks to rule out pseudoprogression before any treatment modification.
options.B: Continue T-DXd and add SBRT to the two oligoprogressive liver lesions as local ablation rather than declaring systemic failure.
options.C: Discontinue T-DXd and switch to tucatinib, trastuzumab, and capecitabine based on HER2CLIMB trial evidence after prior HER2-directed therapy.
options.D: Discontinue T-DXd and start CDK4/6 inhibitor plus aromatase inhibitor plus trastuzumab to prioritize HR+ biology and reduce further chemotherapy.
options.E: Add capecitabine to ongoing T-DXd as combination intensification strategy to overcome local resistance at the two progressive liver lesions.

question: A 49-year-old woman with HR+/HER2+ metastatic breast cancer received: neoadjuvant TCHP (carboplatin+paclitaxel+trastuzumab+pertuzumab), residual disease at surgery. Adjuvant 14 cycles of Trastuzumab emtansine (T-DM1). Progression 13 month later, so started first-line metastatic: trastuzumab + pertuzumab + taxane, progression after 8 month, second-line metastatic: trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd). After 11 months of response on T-DXd, she developed oligoprogressive liver metastases treated with SBRT. T-DXd was continued.Three months later, imaging showed progressive mediastinal and retroperitoneal lymph node metastases, the other metastases were stable. Biopsy of a progressing lymph node confirms ER 70%, PR 40%, HER2 IHC 3+. What is the most appropriate next management?

options.A: Continue T-DXd and administer radiation therapy to the involved lymph nodes
options.B: switch to Trastuzumab Emtansin
options.C: Continue T-DXd and add endocrine therapy + CDK4/6 inhibitor
options.D: Switch to margetuximab + chemotherapy
options.E: Switch to tucatinib + trastuzumab + capecitabine

question: A 34-year-old woman presents with inflammatory changes of the right breast. Clinical staging: cT4b cN3a, skin edema and ulceration, fixed ipsilateral supraclavicular lymph node. Core biopsy (open biopsy from breast mass): invasive NST, ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 0 (IHC 0), grade 3\. Axillary lymph node biopsy: Invasive carcinoma, ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 IHC 1+ Staging CT and bone scan: No distant metastases. ECOG 0\. What is the most appropriate initial systemic treatment?

options.A: Neoadjuvant dose-dense EC followed by carboplatin/paclitaxel plus pembrolizumab
options.B: Neoadjuvant dose-dense AC followed by paclitaxel without immunotherapy
options.C: Neoadjuvant trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd) as primary systemic therapy
options.D: Neoadjuvant trastuzumab + pertuzumab + taxane for HER2-targeted therapy
options.E: Immediate modified radical mastectomy followed by adjuvant chemotherapy

question: A 34-year-old woman with cT4b cN3a G3 triple-negative breast cancer (ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 0 in breast; HER2 1+ in axillary node) began neoadjuvant therapy with: dose-dense 4 cycles of Epirubicin + cyclophosphamide (EC) in combination pembrolizumab. After 3 cycles, clinical examination and imaging show: enlargement of the primary breast mass, increased skin involvement, enlarged supraclavicular lymph nodes, no distant metastases on staging. ECOG 0\. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Continue EC + pembrolizumab for 1–2 additional cycles and reassess
options.B: Proceed immediately to modified radical mastectomy
options.C: Switch to sacituzumab govitecan
options.D: Switch to carboplatin + paclitaxel + pembrolizumab
options.E: Initiate trastuzumab deruxtecan due to HER2 1+ in the lymph node

question: A 34-year-old woman with cT4b cN3a G3 triple-negative breast cancer (ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 0 in breast; HER2 1+ in axillary node) began neoadjuvant therapy with epirubicin + cyclophosphamide + pembrolizumab. After clinical progression, she was switched to: Carboplatin + paclitaxel + pembrolizumab. After 3 cycles of this regimen, she again demonstrates: further enlargement of the breast mass, persistent inflammatory skin changes, progressive supraclavicular lymphadenopathy but no distant metastases on repeat staging. ECOG 0\. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Switch to sacituzumab govitecan in an attempt to achieve tumor shrinkage prior to surgery
options.B: Continue pembrolizumab monotherapy while expediting surgery scheduling
options.C: Proceed to urgent modified radical mastectomy with axillary dissection followed by comprehensive radiation
options.D: Switch to cisplatin monotherapy due to suspected platinum sensitivity in young TNBC
options.E: Enroll in a clinical trial of neoadjuvant antibody–drug conjugate therapy

question: A 34-year-old woman with cT4b cN3a G3 triple-negative breast cancer progressed during sequential neoadjuvant 3 cycles of EC + pembrolizumab and 3 cycles Carboplatin + paclitaxel + pembrolizumab. She underwent modified radical mastectomy with axillary dissection. Pathology shows: ypT3 ypN3a (9/20 positive lymph nodes), M0 L1 V0 Pn0 R1(lymphangiosis medial/dorsal) ER 0%, PR 0%, HER2 0\. Postoperative staging shows progressive infraclavicular lymph node metastases. Germline BRCA testing is pending. What is the most appropriate adjuvant systemic management?

options.A: postoperative radiation following by adjuvant capecitabine for 6–8 cycles, though the absolute magnitude of benefit may differ based on individual patient characteristics.
options.B: Administer sacituzumab govitecan for 6 months, with the caveat that implementation requires careful consideration of patient preferences and comorbidities.
options.C: No further systemic therapy; proceed with comprehensive radiation and close surveillance, and clinicians should weigh the risk-benefit ratio in the context of the patient's overall treatment goals.
options.D: Enroll in a clinical trial evaluating adjuvant antibody–drug conjugates; the strength of this recommendation varies across international consensus guidelines.
options.E: Postoperative radiation of the chest wall, lymph node metastases and lymphatic drainage pathways with simultaneous administration of sacituzumab govitecan

question: A 34-year-old woman with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer had the following treatment history: neoadjuvant anthracycline + platinum + taxane + pembrolizumab (primary refractory), followed by surgery and radiation. Metastatic nodal relapse after 10 months so started by sacituzumab govitecan (SG), under SG disease controlled for 8 months. She now presents with progressive contralateral axillary and paraaortic lymph node metastases. Biopsy of a contralateral axilarry node reveals: ER 0%, PR 0% HER2 IHC 0 with faint, incomplete membrane staining in <10% of tumor cells, ISH negative. A second pathology review confirms HER2 ultra-low, not meeting formal HER2-low (1+) criteria. Comprehensive NGS shows no actionable alterations. Germline BRCA testing is negative.ECOG 0.What is the most appropriate next systemic treatment?

options.A: Initiate trastuzumab deruxtecan given emerging data in HER2 ultra-low disease
options.B: Repeat HER2 testing at a third reference laboratory to attempt reclassification as HER2-low
options.C: Enroll in a clinical trial evaluating next-generation antibody–drug conjugates
options.D: Start eribulin as next-line standard chemotherapy
options.E: Rechallenge with sacituzumab govitecan given prior durable disease control

question: A 49-year-old patient with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) is receiving sacituzumab govitecan (SG) in second line. She previously had anthracycline/taxane-based chemotherapy and PD-L1-negative disease. After 6 months of systemic disease control on SG, restaging imaging shows isolated progressive enlargement of ipsilateral subclavian lymph node metastases (SUV increase and short-axis growth from 14 mm to 24 mm). No new distant metastases are detected. The patient is asymptomatic, ECOG 0\. What is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.A: Discontinue SG and switch to eribulin due to RECIST 1.1 progression, as continuation of antibody–drug conjugate therapy beyond radiologic progression is not evidence-based in metastatic TNBC.
options.B: Continue SG and add locoregional radiotherapy to the progressive subclavian lymph node basin as an ablative approach for oligoprogressive disease, aiming to prolong systemic disease control.
options.C: Continue SG without local therapy, as nodal enlargement under antibody–drug conjugate therapy may represent immune-cell infiltration or pseudoprogression rather than true disease progression.
options.D: Interrupt SG and administer definitive-dose radiotherapy to the supraclavicular region, followed by re-staging and reassessment of systemic therapy options prior to resuming sacituzumab govitecan.
options.E: Switch to platinum-based chemotherapy combined with concurrent radiotherapy to enhance radiosensitization in this biologically aggressive TNBC with isolated nodal progression under current therapy.

question: A 46-year-old woman with metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (PD-L1 CPS 15\) was treated with first-line paclitaxel + pembrolizumab,partial response and second- line sacituzumab govitecan. After 11 months on sacituzumab, she develops progressive headaches, diplopia, and nausea. MRI brain reveals 14 supratentorial and infratentorial metastases (largest 2.2 cm), diffuse linear leptomeningeal enhancement along basilar cisterns and nodular deposits in the cauda equina. CSF cytology: positive for malignant cells. Spine MRI: multifocal nodular leptomeningeal disease. CT: stable extracranial disease. ECOG 1\. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: oral dexamethason, twhole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) followed by intrathecal methotrexate
options.B: Stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to all parenchymal metastases plus continuation of sacituzumab govitecan
options.C: starting oral dexamethason and whole brain radiotherapy (WBRT)
options.D: oral dexamethason followed by capecitabine
options.E: intrathecal methotrexate therapy

question: A 49-year-old woman with HER2-positive / HR-negative metastatic breast cancer presents with new-onset focal seizures and mild expressive aphasia. treatment history: Neoadjuvant TCHP → surgery → adjuvant Trastuzumab/Pertuzumab. Metastatic relapse (lung) started first line metastatic treatment with Trastuzumab-Emtansine. Further progression, so switched to trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd). Currently on T-DXd with stable extracranial disease. Current MRI brain shows: 3 enhancing lesions (largest: 2.8 cm (left frontal, cortical/subcortical), others: 0.8 cm and 1.1 cm), moderate vasogenic edema, no midline shift, no leptomeningeal disease. She started on levetiracetam and dexamethasone. What is the most appropriate next management strategy?

options.A: Immediate stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to all lesions while continuing T-DXd
options.B: Switch systemic therapy to tucatinib + trastuzumab + capecitabine and reassess with MRI in 6 weeks
options.C: Surgical resection of the dominant lesion followed by adjuvant SRS to cavity and remaining lesions
options.D: Whole-brain radiotherapy (WBRT) with hippocampal avoidance
options.E: immediate stereotactic radiosurgery to all lesions followed by switch systemic therapy to tucatinib + trastuzumab + capecitabine

question: A 52-year-old woman presents with newly diagnosed de novo metastatic triple-negative breast cancer. Pathology: ER <1%, PR <1%, HER2 0 by IHC PD-L1 positive (CPS \= 19), Germline BRCA1/2 negative. Disease burden: multiple liver metastases, bilateral pulmonary nodules, no CNS metastases. ECOG 1.No autoimmune disease. She has not received prior chemotherapy for metastatic disease and completed adjuvant dose-dense AC→paclitaxel 3 years ago. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line systemic therapy?

options.A: Pembrolizumab + paclitaxel
options.B: Atezolizumab + nab-paclitaxel
options.C: Atezolizumab + paclitaxel
options.D: Olaparib
options.E: Sacituzumab govitecan

question: A 38-year-old woman with stage IIIB triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) is receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy with weekly paclitaxel plus carboplatin. On cycle 2, day 15, routine labs show: ANC: 0.6 × 10⁹/L, hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL, platelets: 180 × 10⁹/L. Normal renal and hepatic function. Temperature 36,8°C.No symptoms, ECOG 0\. She feels well and denies fever, chills, mucositis, cough, dysuria, or other symptoms. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Withhold chemotherapy temporarily; no antibiotics indicated; consider short-course G-CSF if prolonged neutropenia; resume with dose modification or G-CSF secondary prophylaxis.
options.B: Hold chemotherapy, start prophylactic oral antibiotics, and resume treatment when ANC > 1,0 × 10⁹/L, though the absolute magnitude of benefit may differ based on individual patient characteristics.
options.C: Continue chemotherapy with dose reduction because she is afebrile; comparative effectiveness data for this approach versus alternatives are still evolving.
options.D: Permanently discontinue carboplatin and continue paclitaxel alone; this approach has been evaluated in prospective studies with conflicting results; comparative effectiveness data for this approach versus alternatives are still evolving.
options.E: Admit for IV broad-spectrum antibiotics and initiate therapeutic G-CSF; this strategy should be discussed within a multidisciplinary tumor board setting when feasible.

question: A 29-year-old nulliparous woman is diagnosed with stage IIB (T2N1) estrogen receptor–positive, HER2-negative breast cancer. She desires future fertility. Neoadjuvant chemotherapy is planned to begin in 2 weeks. She is concerned about the potential impact of ovarian stimulation on her estrogen-sensitive tumor. Which of the following is the most appropriate fertility preservation strategy in this patient?

options.A: Standard controlled ovarian stimulation without modification, followed by oocyte or embryo cryopreservation
options.B: Ovarian tissue cryopreservation
options.C: Delay chemotherapy for completion of 6 cycles of ovarian stimulation to maximize oocyte yield
options.D: ovarian stimulation followed by oocyte or embryo cryopreservation, and Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonist therapy during chemotherapy
options.E: Ovarian stimulation with concurrent letrozole, followed by oocyte or embryo cryopreservation

question: A 46-year-old woman with stage II HER2-positive breast cancer is scheduled to receive adjuvant epirubicin plus cyclophosphamide (EC) followed by paclitaxel and trastuzumab. The EC regimen is classified as highly emetogenic chemotherapy (HEC). She has a history of motion sickness but no prior chemotherapy exposure. Which of the following is the most appropriate antiemetic regimen for prevention of acute and delayed chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) during her EC cycles?

options.A: Palonosetron + dexamethasone + aprepitant + olanzapine
options.B: Ondansetron + dexamethasone + fosaprepitant + olanzapine
options.C: Palonosetron + dexamethasone + aprepitant
options.D: Ondansetron + dexamethasone + aprepitant
options.E: Palonosetron + dexamethasone + netupitant + olanzapine

question: A 34-year-old premenopausal woman with newly diagnosed stage II, ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer is planned to receive adjuvant chemotherapy with dose-dense AC followed by paclitaxel. She desires future fertility. You plan ovarian function suppression with a GnRH agonist for ovarian protection during chemotherapy. When should ovarian suppression be initiated to achieve optimal ovarian protection?

options.A: at least 1-2 days before the first chemotherapy cycle
options.B: after computation of the first chemotherapy cycle
options.C: at least 1-2 weeks prior to initiation of chemotherapy
options.D: at least 4 weeks prior to initiation of chemotherapy
options.E: on the same day as the first chemotherapy infusion

question: A 38-year-old premenopausal woman with stage IIIC (cT2N2) hormone receptor–positive, HER2-negative breast cancer is planned for dose-dense ETC (Epirubicin, Paclitaxel, Cyclophosphamide) as neoadjuvant chemotherapy due to 4 biopsy-proven positive axillary lymph nodes.Which of the following additional medications is most important to administer during the cyclophosphamide-containing cycles?

options.A: Aggressive oral hydration with bladder protection measures
options.B: Ciprofloxacin d2-d15 for antibacterial prophylaxis
options.C: G-CSF (pegfilgrastim) for primary neutropenia prophylaxis
options.D: G-CSF (pegfilgrastim) and ciprofloxacin (d2-d15) combined
options.E: Mesna for urothelial protection during cyclophosphamide

question: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman with stage IIA, ER+/PR+, HER2-negative breast cancer is receiving adjuvant anastrozole. After 8 months of therapy, she reports progressive bilateral hand, wrist, and knee pain with morning stiffness lasting 45 minutes. Physical examination reveals tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints without synovitis. ESR and CRP are normal. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative. Vitamin D level is 18 ng/mL (low). Bone mineral density shows osteopenia. She has been compliant with therapy and is concerned about stopping treatment due to fear of recurrence. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: pause anastrozole, start with duloxetin therapy and restart when symptoms are better
options.B: Initiate vitamin D supplementation, structured exercise program, and NSAIDs while continuing anastrozole
options.C: Permanently discontinue anastrozole and initiate tamoxifen
options.D: Start low-dose prednisone for presumed inflammatory arthritis
options.E: pause anastrozole, initiate vitamin D supplementation and re-start again after symptoms getting better

question: A 56-year-old postmenopausal woman with ER-positive early-stage breast cancer is receiving adjuvant letrozole. She reports severe vasomotor symptoms (≥10 hot flashes/day) significantly impairing sleep and quality of life. She has no history of depression. Her oncologic treatment must continue. Which of the following is the most evidence-based and guideline-supported management option?

options.A: Initiate systemic hormone replacement therapy (estrogen ± progestin)
options.B: switch to tamoxifen
options.C: Recommend black cohosh or soy isoflavones as first-line therapy, continue with letrozole
options.D: Continue letrozole and start fezolinetant
options.E: Start venlafaxine

question: A 55-year-old woman with stage IIIA triple-negative breast cancer is receiving adjuvant dose-dense chemotherapy after surgery. She has researched complementary medicine and requests subcutaneous mistletoe (Viscum album) therapy to “strengthen her immune system” and reduce recurrence risk. She states she is willing to pay out of pocket and asks whether you will prescribe or supervise the treatment. Which of the following is the most ethically appropriate response?

options.A: Redirect the patient to another provider without further discussion to avoid medico-legal liability, reputational risk, and institutional scrutiny associated with supervising any complementary or integrative therapy during active oncologic treatment.
options.B: Strongly discourage mistletoe therapy by stating that published data demonstrate an increased recurrence risk, even though existing clinical trial evidence remains inconclusive and no guideline currently identifies mistletoe as harmful to oncologic outcomes.
options.C: Agree to prescribe mistletoe therapy and present it as a likely beneficial adjunct to chemotherapy, reasoning that patient autonomy and willingness to self-fund override physician concerns about the current limitations in the evidence base for this intervention.
options.D: Explain the evidence transparently including the absence of proven survival benefit, discuss potential harms and financial costs, affirm that chemotherapy must not be compromised, and engage in shared decision-making that respects autonomy while maintaining clinical integrity.
options.E: Decline to discuss mistletoe therapy in any clinical context as it falls outside evidence-based oncology guidelines and engaging with the request risks implying legitimacy to an unproven intervention that should not occupy valuable consultation time.

question: A 58-year-old postmenopausal woman with a history of estrogen receptor–positive breast cancer is receiving adjuvant therapy with letrozole. She presents with severe vaginal dryness, dyspareunia, and recurrent irritation. She has tried over-the-counter lubricants with minimal relief. Pelvic exam shows atrophic vaginal mucosa consistent with genitourinary syndrome of menopause. What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: discontinue endocrine therapy and the symptoms will resolve spontaneously
options.B: start with vaginal moisturizers daily in combination with pelvic floor physical therapy
options.C: start daily local vaginal estriol cream for maximum 4 weeks and continue 3 times a week for further 8 weeks
options.D: switch treatment to tamoxifen
options.E: switch treatment to another endocrine therapy like anstrozole and start with vaginal moisturizers daily

question: Which of the following clinical scenarios represents an appropriate indication to recommend initiation of bone-modifying agent therapy in a patient with breast cancer?

options.A: A 52-year-old perimenopausal woman with early-stage ER+/HER2− breast cancer receiving adjuvant endocrine therapy alone with a normal bone mineral density score on baseline DXA scan
options.B: A 48-year-old premenopausal woman with stage II ER+/HER2− breast cancer receiving adjuvant tamoxifen monotherapy with a normal bone mineral density score on baseline DXA scan
options.C: A 63-year-old postmenopausal woman with metastatic ER+/HER2− breast cancer with radiologically confirmed bone metastases identified on staging CT and radionuclide bone scan
options.D: A 44-year-old premenopausal woman with stage II ER+/HER2− breast cancer receiving adjuvant chemotherapy alone with a normal bone mineral density score on baseline DXA scan
options.E: A 59-year-old postmenopausal woman with metastatic ER+/HER2− breast cancer with disease limited to hepatic and pulmonary sites with no evidence of bone involvement

question: A 52-year-old woman with stage II triple-negative breast cancer receives dose-dense doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide followed by paclitaxel. After cycle 3, her LVEF declines from 62% to 47%, but she remains asymptomatic. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Continue anthracycline therapy with close monitoring
options.B: Start dexrazoxane and continue doxorubicin
options.C: Discontinue anthracycline and refer to cardiologist
options.D: Switch to pembrolizumab therapy
options.E: Add beta-blocker and ACE-inhibitor prophylactically and complete planned cycles

question: A patient receiving weekly paclitaxel develops grade 2 peripheral neuropathy interfering with fine motor skills. What is the best management?

options.A: Continue same dose until the neuropathy develops grade 3
options.B: Switch to nab-paclitaxel
options.C: Start vitamin B6 supplementation
options.D: Start duloxetin and continue full dose
options.E: Reduce dose or hold treatment until improvement

question: A 60-year-old woman receiving adjuvant trastuzumab for HER2+ breast cancer develops an asymptomatic LVEF decline from 58% to 44% on routine monitoring. No heart failure symptoms. No prior anthracycline cardiotoxicity. What is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Permanently discontinue trastuzumab; this LVEF decline indicates irreversible cardiotoxicity.
options.B: Continue trastuzumab unchanged and add an ACE inhibitor; asymptomatic decline does not meet interruption criteria.
options.C: Continue trastuzumab and add dexrazoxane to prevent further LVEF decline during ongoing therapy.
options.D: Continue trastuzumab at extended six-weekly intervals to reduce cumulative cardiac exposure while maintaining efficacy.
options.E: Hold trastuzumab and repeat echocardiography in four weeks with cardiology input per guideline criteria.

question: A 47-year-old woman with node-positive HR+/HER2− breast cancer receives adjuvant dose-dense EC with pegfilgrastim given 24 hours post-chemotherapy. On day 10 of cycle 2 she develops fever 38.9°C, ANC 90/µL, BP 105/65 mmHg, and mild oral mucositis. What is the most appropriate immediate management step?

options.A: Admit to hospital, start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate, and reassess ANC and vital signs within 24 hours before escalating therapy
options.B: Admit to hospital, initiate intravenous anti-pseudomonal antibiotics within one hour, and obtain blood cultures before starting antimicrobial therapy
options.C: Admit to hospital, administer an additional dose of pegfilgrastim, and initiate broad-spectrum oral antibiotics pending culture results
options.D: Admit to hospital, initiate intravenous anti-pseudomonal antibiotics, and reduce subsequent EC doses by 25% after recovery to prevent recurrence
options.E: Admit to hospital, add daily short-acting filgrastim to augment granulocyte recovery, and initiate empiric antifungal therapy given the presence of oral mucositis

question: ECG shows QTc 520 ms in a patient on ribociclib. What is the best management in this situation?

options.A: continue therapy in dose reduction
options.B: switch to abemaciclib
options.C: start beta blocker
options.D: hold drug and correct electrolytes
options.E: observe and repeat ECG in 2 weeks

question: A 48-year-old woman on tamoxifen develops proximal deep vein thrombosis. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Continue tamoxifen with concurrent therapeutic anticoagulation.
options.B: Switch to aromatase inhibitor monotherapy and initiate therapeutic anticoagulation.
options.C: Reduce tamoxifen to 10 mg daily and add prophylactic anticoagulation.
options.D: Discontinue tamoxifen and start therapeutic anticoagulation alone.
options.E: Start therapeutic anticoagulation and switch to aromatase inhibitor plus ovarian suppression.

question: A 62-year-old woman with metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer is receiving trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd). After cycle 5 she reports new dry cough and mild dyspnea for 3 days. Vitals: Temp 37.4°C, SpO₂ 95% room air, RR 18/min labs: mild CRP elevation, normal leukocytes, no neutropenia What is the most appropriate diagnostic management step?

options.A: chest x-ray and start oral bread-spectrum antibiotics
options.B: computed tomography of the chest and start prednisone 1mg/kg/day immediately if the imaging suspects T-DXd-related ILD
options.C: wait and watch, control the labs in a week
options.D: computed tomography, perform bronchoscopy with BAL before initiating prednisone 1mg/kg/day
options.E: no imaging diagnostic needed. Postpone the next cycle until the symptoms have subsided.

question: A 59-year-old woman with metastatic HER2-positive breast cancer develops grade 3 ILD attributed to trastuzumab deruxtecan (T-DXd). T-DXd is permanently discontinued. Prednisone 1 mg/kg/day (60 mg daily) is initiated with a taper planned over 8 weeks. She has no history of diabetes, peptic ulcer disease, or anticoagulation. BMI is 27 kg/m². Baseline labs are normal. Which of the following adjunctive measures is important to initiate at the start of corticosteroid therapy?

options.A: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis plus calcium and vitamin D supplementation
options.B: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis for Pneumocystis jirovecii prevention
options.C: Pantoprazole prophylaxis for gastric protection during prolonged corticosteroids
options.D: Weekly blood glucose monitoring for corticosteroid-induced hyperglycemia
options.E: Amoxicillin-sulbactam prophylaxis for broad-spectrum bacterial coverage

question: A 62-year-old woman with HR+/HER2– metastatic breast cancer is receiving palbociclib plus letrozole as first-line therapy. On cycle 2 day 15, routine labs show: ANC: 620/µL, hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL, platelets: 210,000/µL, afebrile, asymptomatic, no signs of infection. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: Continue palbociclib at the same dose and repeat CBC in one week
options.B: Discontinue palbociclib permanently and switch to abemaciclib
options.C: Hold palbociclib and administer prophylactic G-CSF
options.D: Hold palbociclib until ANC ≥1,000/µL, then resume at the same dose
options.E: Continue palbociclib and initiate empiric oral antibiotics

question: A 55-year-old woman with HR+/HER2– metastatic breast cancer is starting first-line endocrine therapy plus a CDK4/6 inhibitor. She has: baseline QTc 472 ms, chronic IBS with intermittent diarrhea, mild baseline neutropenia (ANC 1,400/µL), normal liver function tests. Which of the following comparative toxicity statements regarding ribociclib, palbociclib, and abemaciclib is most accurate?

options.A: Ribociclib and palbociclib have comparable Grade 3–4 neutropenia rates, while abemaciclib causes less neutropenia but higher Grade ≥3 diarrhea and transaminase elevation; QT prolongation is primarily associated with ribociclib.
options.B: All three CDK4/6 inhibitors produce similar Grade 3–4 neutropenia; ribociclib uniquely causes cumulative marrow suppression requiring G-CSF prophylaxis, and QT prolongation risk is equivalent between ribociclib and palbociclib.
options.C: Ribociclib has the highest hepatotoxicity incidence among the three agents, while palbociclib and abemaciclib have comparable gastrointestinal toxicity profiles; QT prolongation is a class effect of CDK6 inhibition.
options.D: bemaciclib and palbociclib have similar Grade ≥3 diarrhea rates due to continuous dosing, whereas ribociclib causes more frequent QT prolongation but produces less severe hematologic toxicity than the other two agents.
options.E: Neutropenia is dose-cumulative and cytotoxic with all three agents; abemaciclib has the highest gastrointestinal toxicity overall due to its greater CDK4 selectivity relative to palbociclib and ribociclib.

question: A 58-year-old woman with germline BRCA1–mutated, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer achieved partial remission after first-line carboplatin/paclitaxel. She transitioned to maintenance olaparib and has remained on therapy for 14 months. Her prior treatment history includes: Dose-dense anthracycline/taxane in the (neo)adjuvant setting 6 years earlier and carboplatin-based chemotherapy for metastatic disease. Baseline blood counts prior to olaparib: Hb: 12.8 g/dL, ANC: 3.4 × 10⁹/L, platelets: 240 × 10⁹/L. Current laboratory findings: Hb: 8.4 g/dL, MCV: 108 fL, reticulocyte count: low, ANC: 1.5 × 10⁹/L, platelets: 135 × 10⁹/L. LDH, bilirubin, haptoglobin: normal. Vitamin B12, folate, and iron studies: normal Peripheral smear shows macrocytosis and occasional hypogranulated neutrophils but no circulating blasts. Olaparib dose was reduced 6 weeks ago without hematologic improvement. Which of the following is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?

options.A: Initiate erythropoiesis-stimulating agent therapy and reassess blood counts in 4 weeks.
options.B: Measure serum erythropoietin level and repeat reticulocyte count before proceeding.
options.C: Perform bone marrow biopsy with cytogenetics and myeloid next-generation sequencing panel.
options.D: Permanently discontinue olaparib and monitor blood counts for spontaneous recovery.
options.E: Begin empiric vitamin B12 supplementation despite currently normal serum levels.

question: Which biomarker should be assessed at first diagnosis of hormone receptor positive metastatic breast cancer?

options.A: Estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, HER2 status by IHC/ISH, and PD-L1 status at initial metastatic diagnosis without genomic or germline testing.
options.B: Estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, HER2 by IHC/ISH, PIK3CA mutation, PD-L1 status, and ESR1 mutational status after prior aromatase inhibitor.
options.C: Estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, germline BRCA1/2, PD-L1 status, and HER2-low status by IHC/ISH at the time of metastatic diagnosis.
options.D: Estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, PIK3CA mutation, AKT1/PTEN status, and ESR1 mutational status after prior aromatase inhibitor treatment.
options.E: Estrogen receptor, progesterone receptor, PIK3CA mutation, AKT1/PTEN status, ESR1 mutational status after AI treatment, and HER2-ultralow status by IHC.

question: In mTNBC, which PD-L1 testing approach is validated for selecting patients for first-line pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy?

options.A: Tumor proportion score (TPS) ≥1% using 22C3
options.B: Combined positive score (CPS) ≥10 using 22C3
options.C: Immune cell (IC) ≥1% using SP142
options.D: TPS ≥50% using SP263
options.E: Combined positive score (CPS) ≥15 using 22C4

question: Which biomarker best predicts benefit from sacituzumab govitecan in heavily pretreated mTNBC?

options.A: High TROP-2 expression by immunohistochemistry
options.B: SLFN11 overexpression by transcriptomic assay
options.C: High Ki-67 proliferation index on core biopsy
options.D: Activating TROP-2 mutation on next-generation sequencing
options.E: No validated predictive biomarker selection required

question: A patient with mTNBC received first-line chemotherapy. No prior comprehensive NGS was performed. She now progresses. When should PIK3CA/PTEN/AKT testing be performed?

options.A: Only after progression on three or more lines of therapy
options.B: Only if PD-L1 combined positive score is 10 or higher
options.C: At first progression if comprehensive testing was not done previously
options.D: Only within the context of a registered clinical trial
options.E: Only if tumor is classified as HER2-low on repeat biopsy

question: In the Phase III SOLAR-1 trial, which finding regarding PIK3CA mutation detection in liquid biopsy (ctDNA) versus archived tissue was most significant for clinical practice?

options.A: ctDNA results were non-concordant with tissue in over 50% of the metastatic cohort, limiting its utility as a companion diagnostic.
options.B: Tissue testing was significantly more sensitive than ctDNA for detecting H1047R across all metastatic subgroups analyzed.
options.C: PIK3CA mutations in ctDNA were only predictive of alpelisib response when variant allele frequency exceeded 5% at baseline.
options.D: ctDNA testing identified patients who derived clinical benefit from alpelisib despite having PIK3CA wildtype results on archived tissue.
options.E: ctDNA results matched archived tissue PIK3CA testing in approximately 80% of the metastatic cohort across all mutation hotspots.

question: A 49-year-old woman is diagnosed with stage II (T2N1) triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC). The oncology team is considering neoadjuvant systemic therapy. hich of the following statements regarding PD-L1 testing in early-stage TNBC is most accurate?

options.A: PD-L1 testing is not required to select patients for neoadjuvant pembrolizumab in high-risk early-stage TNBC.
options.B: PD-L1 positivity defined by CPS ≥10 using the 22C3 assay is required before adding pembrolizumab to neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
options.C: PD-L1 testing using the SP142 assay is mandatory because only PD-L1–positive tumors benefit from immune checkpoint inhibition in early TNBC.
options.D: PD-L1 testing should be performed on surgical specimens rather than core biopsies to accurately determine eligibility for immunotherapy.
options.E: PD-L1 expression assessed by tumor proportion score (TPS) ≥5% predicts pathologic complete response benefit in early TNBC.

question: A 35-year-old G1P0 woman at 18 weeks of gestation presents with a 2.8 cm palpable mass in the right breast. Core needle biopsy reveals: Invasive ductal carcinoma, ER 80%, PR 60%, HER2 negative, Ki-67 25% Breast ultrasound confirms the lesion. Axillary ultrasound shows one abnormal lymph node, biopsy-proven metastatic carcinoma. No evidence of distant metastasis.The patient strongly desires to continue the pregnancy. What is the most appropriate management?

options.A: neoadjuvant anthracycline-based chemotherapy following by surgery
options.B: Modified radical mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection during pregnancy, followed by adjuvant chemotherapy in the second trimester
options.C: Breast-conserving surgery with sentinel lymph node biopsy during pregnancy, followed by adjuvant chemotherapy in the second trimester and radiation therapy after delivery
options.D: Neoadjuvant endocrine therapy until delivery, followed by surgery postpartum
options.E: Immediate breast-conserving surgery with sentinel lymph node biopsy followed by adjuvant radiation therapy during pregnancy

question: A 34-year-old woman, G2P1, at 11 weeks’ gestation is diagnosed with stage II (cT2N1) triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma. She is otherwise healthy and strongly desires to continue the pregnancy if medically reasonable. Staging workup shows no distant metastases.Which of the following is the most appropriate management recommendation?

options.A: Begin chemotherapy immediately at 11 weeks, accepting first-trimester organogenesis risk to avoid any delay in treatment.
options.B: Postpone all oncologic treatment until after delivery to fully eliminate fetal exposure to chemotherapy throughout the entire pregnancy.
options.C: Recommend immediate pregnancy termination, as first-trimester chemotherapy is absolutely contraindicated and continuing the pregnancy significantly worsens overall maternal survival.
options.D: Delay chemotherapy until the second trimester (after 12–14 weeks), proceed with surgery if indicated, and monitor under multidisciplinary care.
options.E: Administer endocrine therapy throughout pregnancy as a safer systemic alternative to cytotoxic chemotherapy until delivery.

question: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a 2.5 cm left axillary lymph node. Core biopsy shows metastatic adenocarcinoma: ER 95%, PR 80%, HER2 0, GATA3 positive, Ki-67 20%, Grade 2. Breast mammography, ultrasound, and MRI reveal no identifiable primary lesion. CT shows no distant disease. She undergoes axillary lymph node dissection with 4 of 12 nodes positive, largest deposit 2.8 cm, with extranodal extension. What is the most appropriate adjuvant systemic and locoregional treatment strategy?

options.A: Regional nodal irradiation followed by anthracycline/taxane chemotherapy and endocrine therapy plus CDK4/6 inhibitor, as four positive nodes with extranodal extension meets monarchE eligibility criteria mandating addition.
options.B: Skin-sparing mastectomy followed by regional nodal irradiation, then anthracycline/taxane chemotherapy and endocrine therapy, as surgical removal of the occult primary site is required before initiating systemic therapy.
options.C: Regional nodal irradiation to axilla, supraclavicular fossa, and internal mammary chain, followed by anthracycline/taxane chemotherapy and aromatase inhibitor, treating this GATA3-positive occult presentation as breast primary.
options.D: Platinum-based chemotherapy per carcinoma of unknown primary protocol, as the absence of a confirmed breast lesion on multimodality imaging precludes establishing a definitive breast primary diagnosis.
options.E: Adjuvant aromatase inhibitor alone for 5–10 years, as the absence of an identifiable primary and favorable receptor profile make chemotherapy unnecessary regardless of nodal burden or extranodal extension.

question: A 78-year-old woman is diagnosed with invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast. Pathology reveals a 2.8 cm tumor, estrogen receptor (ER) positive, progesterone receptor (PR) positive, HER2-negative. Sentinel lymph node biopsy shows 2 of 4 nodes positive. She undergoes breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy) with clear margins.She has well-controlled hypertensionand mild osteoarthritis but is otherwise functionally independent. Which of the following is the most appropriate adjuvant treatment approach for this patient?

options.A: Endocrine therapy alone without radiation
options.B: Radiation therapy plus endocrine therapy
options.C: Chemotherapy followed by endocrine therapy and ribociclib
options.D: Radiation therapy alone
options.E: Radiation therapy, endocrine therapy, and adjuvant ribociclib

question: In Germany a 54 year old postmenopausal woman is diagnosed with invasive breast carcinoma. Initial staging shows: cT2 cN0, M1, ER: 15 %(IRS:2), PR:0%, HER2 IHC 2+, FISH negative. Bone metastases involving T1, T2, and T9 vertebrae. No visceral metastases. ECOG performance status 0\. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial systemic treatment strategy?

options.A: Platinum-based Chemotherapy + Bevacizumab + bone-target agents
options.B: Aromatase inhibitor + CDK4/6 inhibitor + bone target agents
options.C: Lumpectomy + SLNE and radiation therapy for bone metastases
options.D: Trastuzumab + pertuzumab + docetaxel
options.E: Trastuzumab emtansin (T-DM1) + bone target agents

question: A 67-year-old postmenopausal woman undergoes screening mammography that reveals 1.5 cm grouped amorphous calcifications in the left breast. Stereotactic core needle biopsy shows: Ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), grade 1, ER 95%, PR 90%, HER2 negative. No necrosis. No microinvasion. Radiologic–pathologic concordance confirmed. Breast MRI shows no additional lesions. Clinical breast exams are normal. She has well-controlled hypertension and diabetes. Which is the most appropriate management recommendation?

options.A: Breast-conserving surgery alone, without any form of adjuvant radiation therapy.
options.B: Active monitoring with mammography of the affected side every 6 months ± endocrine therapy.
options.C: Breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy) followed by adjuvant whole-breast radiation therapy.
options.D: Total mastectomy with sentinel lymph node biopsy to ensure complete and definitive locoregional surgical staging.
options.E: Active monitoring with mammography of the affected side every 12 months ± endocrine therapy.
